speaker: interviewer
text: --of an interview with Dr. Vera Laska. Dr. Laska, you were describing what happened when you were in the bar-- barn and you were discovered. What, what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened next, four and a half long days later-- meanwhile, by, by the way, all the time I heard shooting. But I couldn't tell-- right, left, up, down-- shooting and all kinds of shooting. I cannot tell what's shot from a revolver or a gun or, or something bigger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the war somehow went over that village. And after four and a half days, Ukraine-- Ukrainian forced labor came down to, to the barn and started hollering, woman, come down. War kaput, Hitler, kaput! OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: And first I didn't believe it, but then he said, woman, come down, war kaput, I have bread. I came down. [LAUGHS] It is as simple as that. Four and half days. And before that, you know, I was hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I-- we exchanged a few rather interesting words. And he went that way and I went that way. He was still afraid of his German farmer boss, and he said he has to go now. And I said, where do I go now? So I start, he said, the soldiers are there, I'll go there. So I went that way. And suddenly I see there were Russian soldiers with Russian machinery, which I immediately knew, of course, because they had the five-edge star. And five-edge star to me was communist. Except they were Americans, which, idiotically, the American army had the Russian star in white. Well, who knew? White or red, it was the five-edge star. So I somehow managed to make the-- make the few steps there. And practically-- I didn't collapse, but I practically collapsed. And I was blabbering the few words of Russian, saying, water, water in Russian. I figured same as in Czech, [CZECH] [CZECH]. And they looked at me like, I don't know, the Loch Ness Monster or something. So that's how I liberated myself, or that's how the Americans liberated me, if you wish. Do you have any idea where you were, what the nearest town was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, unfortunately. [SIGHS] Every time I think of that, I could kick myself. Well, this was in a meadow, but I don't know next to the village. Next to that village where I, I ran away from the potato-- from the boiled potatoes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That American officer was eating a beautiful red apple. And I just grabbed it practically from his mouth. I ate it. He looked strangely. That was his first encounter. And then later on we talked. And he said, I didn't know that women were in concentration camps. So, New Yorker. Yeah, time. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your medical condition like at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Medical?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, what were you physically--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was about-- I weighed about 90 pounds. I rattled when I walked, on the bones. But I felt fit and I evidently-- I had some trouble, you know, neglected teeth. But no TB, no other trouble. We all had big physical checkups when we got home, and nothing was wrong. Knock on wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I went back to Prague, you know, home grounds.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Well, I, I met up with some-- some resistors-- former resistors from Prague at Dora. And they were-- two of them were doctors. So they got a brilliant idea of organizing a car-- organizing, by the way, means to steal, OK-- organizing a car. And they also organized some painter who could paint-- you know, very official-looking, beautiful letters-- Czechoslovak Red Cross on top, on four sides, or two sides. Then we went to the Dora official of the SS. This was very freshly at the end of the war in that region-- officially not yet the end of the war, April. And we got all the files into the car. And there was one, two, three-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three of them and me is four, and one more guy I don't remember-- the five of us, we didn't fit the car. So the two of them organized motorcycles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were two motorcycles and Czechoslovak Red Cross car. You know, the two motorcycles played escort. And we just retraced Prague that way, you know, and we went. And we got through to every American checkpoint-- you know, they salute the Czech Red Cross-- until we got to a Czech border and there were the Russkies and say, what Czech Red Cross, [LAUGHS] you know. Papers? We had no papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, luckily one of those doctors was smart, and he had some-- I don't know, booze, vodka-- not vodka-- whatever they sell in Germany-- and a couple of bottles to the Ivans and we went through. And by 9th of May, we were in Prague. The uprising just ended in Prague. We came for the tail end. And from then on, the story takes a very happy turn, in the sense that pretty soon I got-- well, I worked for the war crime investigation, which was interesting, for about a year. And then my professor said, we'll send you to America on a scholarship. And that's where the-- the long, happy end follows, you know, in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me--I'm going to take you back just a moment. You came to Prague, and how did you get the job with the war crimes investigation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, because this doctor who was the head honcho in this Czechoslovak [LAUGHS] Red Cross venture was a friend of the head of the Czech war crime commission, which was by then founded-- or founded in those days. And he was an ex-concentration camp person also, of course. He later ended up as-- in the United-- employee of the United Nations, as the governor of Madagascar, a Bohunk or a Czech-- I shouldn't say Bohunk-- a Czech governor of Madagascar for many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CZECH]. And retired to a farm in Vermont. But he is dead too by now, unfortunately. Anyhow, so it was this concentration camp group, really, most of us who were in the war crime investigation. And so were the members of the French and Norwegian, Danish, whatever-- war crime investigation teams. 99% ex-concentration camp people. The only reliable people at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were working in Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were gathering testimonials-- or testimonies, testimonies, from former prisoners about the SS in Dachau, Buchenwald, what have you. We didn't do Auschwitz, I wonder why. Anyhow, I remember Buchen-- Dachau first-- that was the first camp-- Buchenwald and some others-- Sachsenhausen, Sachsenhausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your role?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the executive secretary, believe it or not. I had an office, huge. And in the corner was a desk. And there I sat in eyeglasses, which I didn't need, and always black or dark blue, so I would look older. But I had a very good, beautiful team who helped me out. I don't know, maybe they just wanted a woman as executive secretary, I don't know. And of course the board of directors met every week, 12 of them. And those were just about all, again, ex-concentration-- Dachau, Buchenwald, Sachsenhausen, Neuengamme or whatever. You know, Auschwitz. And there were some high military officials, and there was one judge. There was Milada Horakova. If there was another woman I don't remember. I was so taken by Dr. Milada Horakova, who was a member of parliament by that time, who was in Ravensbruck during that war. And then after the Communists came in, she was executed, in the big Slansky trials of the early '50s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of which trials they were preparing for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, of course. Because I was sort of calling the shots and organizing things, stuff like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any, any-- who was being tried?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I don't remember. Because there were people from camps where I haven't been. And even when-- for instance, I never knew names of guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't say, what's your name, guard? You got a slap in the face. And why would they tell you? And why would they tell you their real name?

speaker: interviewee
text: People know the name of Mengele. And I would bet more than, than, than a nickel that most people who say, I remember Mengele, they don't remember him. They remember an-- an officer with the riding crop who stood there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't think they-- because when we arrived, there were officers. How the hell do you know it was Mengele? Very few people really knew Mengele. There were some prisoners, doctors for instance, when he came to do his experiments in the, in the hospital, they knew this is Dr. Mengele. But us peasants in the other, other blocks, very few. What do you remember about taking people's testimonies at that time from Buchenwald and Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would interview them. First of all, we, we interviewed people about the German supervisors, the SS. We also interviewed a few people who claimed that they were in concentration camps-- a new angle for you-- never been there. Maybe they were black marketeers. Maybe they collaborated with the Germans. Maybe they were Sudeten Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those were the easy ones, because we would quiz them. Oh yeah, you were in  Dachau? And I had the plan of Dachau, with all the numbers of the blocks and how many yards and so on. And which block were you in? And what was your number? And who, who was in the next block? And what happened on Thursday, January 5th, you know, and so on. So that was one, one type, it was a sort of a detective work. And the others, oh you know, you knew Supervisor [GERMAN]. OK, what do you know about Supervisor [GERMAN]? He beat people. Did you see it? Yes, I saw it. When was it? How did he beat him? Did he step on the guy's neck? Did he throw him down? OK, sign here. You know, that was that type of interview. Who, who were most of the people you were talking to? Were they also fellow political prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some were. Because they were the ones who were in, in Dachau and Sachsenhausen and so on. We had a separate group of the students, you know, who were in-- mostly in Sachsenhausen but also transferred. And then this was typed up. At that time, everything was-- no Dictaphones, everything. Take notes and usually with me, somebody else also taking notes, so then we put two notes together. And then we had secretaries who typed it up. And the guy had to either wait or come back, sign it, notarize it. Because this is Europe, you know, everything had to be notarized. And then we sent it wherever we sent it--in Germany, where the trials were being-- being prepared at that time. We had piles-- files this high. And then I left for America after a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were also studying and the time, is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you studying?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Charles University.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you arrange to come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I didn't. My professors, bless their soul, decided that I should go to America for one reason-- to study American history and bring it back to become an assistant in American history, in order to balance out the new chair of Soviet history, which was by that time approved, you see. So it was sort of inner politics behind it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so two of us were sent. One went to-- I don't know where he went. Maybe he went to Columbia, and then stayed in the states after the communists took over and taught for many years in Buffalo. What's in Buffalo? University of New York, Buffalo branch or University of Buffalo. I don't know, but it was town of Buffalo.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they asked me where I would like to go, I said I want to go to Chicago. So they sent me to Chicago. Because President Masaryk taught there, you see. And President Benes, when he became an exiled president taught there. So I figured it's good for them, it's good for me. So that's how I got to the University of Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this that you came to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started the winter semester of '47, in Chicago. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And once you were here, what did you study?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- you know, Chicago at that time, full program was three courses. That the semester system. So I took two courses in American history and one for fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first one for fun course I took was American education. And then there was all kinds of other things of that I was interested in, like Chinese history. All I knew was that China is big, and it's somewhere there, you know. And all kinds of other courses. Always two American history, one for fun. Because I had-- well, that's beside the point, OK. And did you earn your degree?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got my degree finally in 1959, got a PhD In American history, with a minor in Latin America.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were also married in America. What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that happened in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Andy. And his last name is Laska, which is the only reason I married the guy. Because Laska means love in Czech. And he is from Cleveland, Ohio.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a son several years later. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son's name was Thomas, Thomas Vaclav. But he died at age 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: In an automobile accident. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we adopted a 12-year-old from Costa Rica. And his name is Paul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he's a Phys Ed major.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to your husband and your sons about your experience-- your wartime experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. I don't think they really, really were interested. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was one chapter that somehow-- not intentionally, but it just so happened it was closed. Don't forget, here was a European girl who came into a miraculous new world, with so many new interests-- American history, which always fascinated me. I mean always, ever since I stuck my nose into it it fascinated me. It still fascinates me. So very little. And there were all kinds of other things to discuss, so very little talk. As a matter of fact, I don't think my son Paul ever read my book. But then he didn't read my book about the women in the revolution or about Benjamin Franklin. He is interested in Phys Ed, so. And my intellectual son was dead. Because I started writing after he died. I didn't write. In '72, I started my column in the local paper. I started reviewing books. And I started writing books, just do something that would take my mind away from that. Because that was much worse than Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the 1970s, you wrote a book about your war experience. What drove you to do that? What motivated you to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, That was in-- that was in the '80s.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the '80s?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my one, two, three, four-- that's my fifth book.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what motivated you to write that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you. Because so many people who don't know beans about the subject teach about Holocaust. And nobody, not even a dog was barking, about the five million non-Jewish people. Resistance, what resistance? You mean the war, the soldiers? No. The national resistance in France, in Norway, in Denmark. There are shelves of books written about each in Europe. And here, nobody knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I dropped what I was doing-- at that time I was doing. And I have about 2/3 ready a biography of Abigail Adams, fascinating woman. And as I was on the floor of my study sorting chapter 15 or whatever, it hit me, you know, like a good Catholics have a vision. I had a vision. I'm an idiot. What am I doing with Abigail Adams? I should write about the women-- nobody is writing about the women. And nobody is writing about the resistance. So that's how the book-- I scooped it together and put it away.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're late.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I know. We are finishing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a few more questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your current profession now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm a, a professor emerita, I teach only-- I'm officially retired from teaching. So I teach only the fall semester. And I have a column in the local papers. So that is basically it. And I lecture quite a bit, mostly about either women or concentration camps, Holocaust, resistance, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking back on your experience, to what do you attribute your survival?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a combination of healthy body-- which I was blessed with at that time-- good luck, result that nothing helped. And in a way, determination, in that order. Or maybe luck comes first. Because without luck, you could be healthy and-- and determined, and you went up the chimney.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wrote at the-- in the epilogue of your book that your memories remain unchanged, that you cultivate them in solitude, because people around you wouldn't comprehend. What-- can you just explain that a little? No, they wouldn't, they wouldn't. You wouldn't, and you work in this field. Because there is something that's very hard to express in books. And Eli Wiesel cannot express it, and Primo Levi cannot express it, as good as they are. And several women who are even better cannot express it. It's the constant feeling in your stomach of fear. I cannot express this either-- express it either. I'm trying to approximate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The smells you cannot reproduce. And the whole atmosphere of this, what I call, phantasmagorical atmosphere. Dante's hell would be nursery school compared with it. To live-- not every day, but every hour, every minute, every second-- that you don't know whether in next moment you won't be marching up the chimney [POINTING] or being shot or die of typhoid fever or something. Nobody can convey that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last question, do you have a message for future generations, based on your own experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes I, I definitely do. And that was the reason why I am teaching a course of the same title as my book is. And maybe if you'll allow me, I will read that from, from the book, all right. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is called And I must show off this fabulous picture of a woman on a swastika, which I think expresses it very well. And what I'm going to read you are just a few sentences from the epilogue to the books-- to the book. 'Celebrate life as much as you can, without hurting others. For too much of it has been wasted through sheer madness. Place flowers of sympathy and sorrow on the non-existent graves of millions who were annihilated through cruelty and with malice of forethought. Keep in mind the victims of the Holocaust and their bones scattered all over Europe, unmarked with crosses or David's stars. For lest you forget, their sacrifices will have been in vain, and humanity will tumble into the abyss of evil again. Honor the memory of those fallen and strengthen your soul against repeated bereavement of mankind, so that you can look with a clear conscience into the eyes of your children. Weep not for the survivors of the purgatory called Holocaust. For theirs is the ultimate glory in triumph. They outlived the evil of their tormentors. And by their very lives, overcame it. Learn from them. For nobody, but nobody, knows how to savor the sunlight and the rain drops, the free flight of the birds and the smile of the flowers, as those of us who were once denied dignity, beauty, and the simplest things in life that make it worth living.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Laska, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are most welcome. My pleasure.
